skill.name: error-tracking
skill.description: Guides teams through Sentry v8 error tracking, error capture, and performance monitoring patterns for Node.js services.
skill.summary: Add Sentry v8 error capture and performance monitoring to service code.
skill.author: diet103

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many critical and high patterns, but review found most were false positives from Markdown code fences, relative import examples, localhost curl examples, and environment variable configuration snippets. The confirmed risk is legitimate but sensitive telemetry guidance: implementations may send exceptions, user IDs, stack traces, and operational context to Sentry if teams do not scrub data.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Error Telemetry Can Expose Sensitive Context
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends Sentry.captureException with route tags, user identifiers, operation context, Sentry DSN configuration, and broad capture of all errors. This is legitimate observability guidance, but it can transmit sensitive user, stack trace, and service data to Sentry unless data scrubbing and consent controls are enforced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples explicitly capture exceptions with user IDs and operational context and configure a Sentry DSN. The skill also warns against sensitive data exposure, so the risk is implementation-dependent rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated external command detections align with Markdown fenced code blocks and illustrative TypeScript snippets. No executable skill script or instruction to run arbitrary user-controlled shell commands was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences and documentation examples, not executable files. The only command examples are localhost curl checks for testing Sentry endpoints.

low_findings.title: Relative Imports and Project Paths Misclassified as Filesystem Abuse
low_findings.description: Path traversal and device-file alerts refer to relative TypeScript imports and documented project paths. They do not direct the assistant to read secrets or access arbitrary files outside the project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The traversal markers appear in normal import paths such as parent-directory module references. The /dev path is a documentation location, not an instruction to access a device file.

low_findings.title: Environment Variables Used for Configuration
low_findings.description: The process.env references are for SENTRY_DSN and NODE_ENV configuration in Sentry initialization examples. This is expected for observability setup, but secrets should remain in environment configuration and should not be logged.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable names and surrounding Sentry.init examples show conventional configuration use. I found no evidence that these variables are printed, exfiltrated outside Sentry setup, or used for arbitrary file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Exception Capture With User Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples encourage capturing all errors and adding user and operation context. This can be appropriate for error tracking, but it is a data exposure pattern if sensitive fields are not filtered before telemetry is sent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The text explicitly requires all errors to be captured and shows user IDs and operation context. The risk is mitigated by later warnings not to expose sensitive data, so it is not a block.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent error capture and performance visibility across services. This skill provides Sentry v8 patterns for controllers, routes, cron jobs, workflows, and database operations.
actual_capabilities: Shows Sentry v8 initialization patterns for Node.js services.
Provides controller, route, workflow, and cron job error capture examples.
Demonstrates performance spans for asynchronous operations and database queries.
Lists local test endpoints for validating Sentry integration behavior.
Includes implementation checklists and common error handling mistakes.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install Sentry packages automatically.
Examples are tailored to specific form and notification service paths.
Teams must configure Sentry DSNs, sampling, and data scrubbing themselves.
Some Sentry API examples may need adjustment for future SDK versions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Service Error Capture
use_cases.description: Apply one Sentry pattern across controllers, routes, and async workflows.

use_cases.title: Monitor Scheduled Jobs
use_cases.description: Instrument cron jobs with spans, error capture, and process exit handling.

use_cases.title: Improve Production Observability
use_cases.description: Add database and operation tracing so slow or failing paths are easier to investigate.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Error Capture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-tracking skill to add Sentry error capture to this route. Preserve the current response behavior and avoid sending sensitive fields.

prompt_templates.title: Instrument a Cron Job
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-tracking skill to instrument this cron job with Sentry v8 spans, exception capture, and clear failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Database Performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-tracking skill to wrap these database calls with performance monitoring and flag slow query paths.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Existing Observability
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the error-tracking skill to review this service for missing Sentry capture, unsafe error context, and untracked async operations.

output_examples.input: A controller catches errors with only console logging.
output_examples.output: A revised controller pattern that captures exceptions in Sentry, keeps the client response stable, and avoids sensitive context.

output_examples.input: A cron job runs without tracing or fatal error reporting.
output_examples.output: A monitoring plan that initializes Sentry first, wraps execution in a span, and reports failures before exiting.

output_examples.input: A service has slow database operations with no visibility.
output_examples.output: A performance tracking approach that names operations, tags model access, and records slow query signals.

best_practices: Scrub passwords, tokens, personal data, and request bodies before sending telemetry.
Import Sentry instrumentation before application code that may throw errors.
Use consistent tags and operation names so incidents can be grouped reliably.
anti_patterns: Do not replace Sentry capture with console.error as the only error handling path.
Do not attach full request payloads or secrets as Sentry context.
Do not enable high sampling rates in production without cost and privacy review.

faq.question: Does this skill install Sentry?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and review guidance for projects that use Sentry v8.

faq.question: Which runtimes does it target?
faq.answer: The examples target Node.js services using the Sentry Node SDK.

faq.question: Can it help with cron jobs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a required pattern for initializing instrumentation and reporting scheduled job failures.

faq.question: Does it cover performance monitoring?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes spans for async operations and database performance tracking guidance.

faq.question: Is sensitive data handled automatically?
faq.answer: No. Developers must configure data scrubbing and avoid adding sensitive fields to Sentry context.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
